petitioner_background: The petitioner is self-represented and filed an employment-based immigrant visa petition seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. No details about the petitioner's field of work, nationality, or specific career qualifications are provided in the decision, as the AAO did not reach the merits of the case. The petition was filed at the Vermont Service Center under receipt number EAC 02 131 50114.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO rejected the appeal as untimely filed without reaching the merits of the extraordinary ability claim. The director issued the unfavorable decision on April 20, 2004, and properly notified the petitioner of the 33-day filing deadline. The petitioner submitted Form I-290B on May 22, 2004, but it was rejected on June 1, 2004 because it was not properly signed. A properly signed Form I-290B was not submitted until June 9, 2004—50 days after the decision was issued—making the appeal untimely. The director declined to treat the late appeal as a motion to reopen or reconsider and forwarded the matter to the AAO, which rejected it.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The appeal was rejected on procedural grounds for being untimely filed. The petitioner's initial Form I-290B submitted on May 22, 2004 was rejected on June 1, 2004 because it was not properly signed, as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(a)(2) and Part 7 of the Form I-290B instructions. The properly signed appeal was not filed until June 9, 2004, which was 50 days after the April 20, 2004 decision—well beyond the 33-day deadline for mailed decisions under 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(2)(i) and 8 C.F.R. § 103.5a(b).
